rulings.Case Title: HADAS GERGIS VS. PAT LEARY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set A Hearing Date For Petitioner'S Ccp 1094.5 Writ Of Administrative Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 4, 2025, line 1, PETITIONER HADAS GERGIS'Motion To Set A Hearing Date For Petitioner'S Ccp 1094.5 Writ Of Administrative Mandate Petitioner's motion to set a hearing date for the writ of administrative mandamus is denied. The court determines that defendant's motion to dismiss per CCP 583.310 and 583.360 (including any tolling issues), should be resolved before the court sets the hearing on the writ for administrative mandamus. If the court dismisses the action, then the court and respondent will not need to exert resources regarding the underlying merits of the dispute. If the court denies the motion to dismiss, then the court will set a merits-briefing schedule, which can be discussed at the hearing on the motion to dismiss on March 4, 2025. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTOPHER BRITT ET AL VS. ASHBURY GENERAL CONTRACTING AND ENGINEERING, A ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 4, 2025, line 2, DEFENDANT ROLLAMATIC ROOFS, INC'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) The motion to quash service of summons on Rollamatic Roofs, Inc. is continued to March 3, 2025 at 9:00 a.m. in Dept. 301. The parties are ordered to complete further briefing as discussed below. This case arises from a construction dispute that involves waterproofing issues. Plaintiffs knew that Rollamatic designed, constructed, and installed a skylight in the property. They commenced litigation against other entities involved in the construction in October 2022 but did not sue Rollamatic. Plaintiffs' evidence establishes that they became aware that Rollamatic had potential liability "[d]uring further repairs in 2024." Kazanovicz Dec. para. 10. They sued Rollamatic in late 2024. Meet and confer letters presented in Rollamatic's declarations appear to establish that some repairs were taking place around the skylight in early 2024. Declarations from both parties do not establish what repairs took place. A plaintiff's leave to amend and name Doe defendants is liberally construed, but a motion to quash service of summons may be denied where the newly named defendant would be prejudiced by delay. See A&N v. County of Los Angeles (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1058. The court does not find persuasive Rollamatic's argument that its motion should be granted merely because Plaintiffs did not name it sooner. But Rollamatic also raises an argument about prejudice, and specifically that destructive testing of the skylight it installed has occurred. As noted above, the court cannot ascertain from the papers what has occurred with the skylight in 2024 or whether Rollamatic is correct. The burden lies with a defendant to show prejudice, but here access to the facts about what destructive testing if any has occurred is exclusively in Plaintiffs' possession. The court therefore orders Plaintiffs to file a supplemental brief of no more than 10 pages with evidence related to this issue within 21 calendar days of notice of entry of this order. Rollamatic may respond in a brief of no more than 10 pages within 7 calendar days of Plaintiffs' supplemental filing. The matter is continued for hearing to March 14, 2025 at 9:00 a.m. in Dept. 301. (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: JASON DEWITT VS. JACINTO JOSE VALDOVINO ET AL **Provide Access**
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 4, 2025, line 5, DEFENDANT JACINTO VALDOVINO #AM3654 **PROVIDE ACCESS** DEMURRER to COMPLAINT (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) The demurrer will be sustained with leave to amend. To prevail on a claim for assault, Plaintiff must show, among other things, that Defendant threatened to touch him in a harmful or offensive manner, and it appeared to Plaintiff that Defendant was about to carry out that threat. (CACI No. 1301.) Words alone do not amount to an assault. (Id.) Plaintiff must allege that in addition to Defendant intending to cause a harmful or offensive contact, or an imminent apprehension thereof, that Plaintiff was put in imminent apprehension of such harmful or offensive contact. See Lowry v. Standard Oil Co. (1944) 63 Cal.App.2d 1, 6-7. Plaintiff alleges a third party, not Defendant Valdovino, called him and stated, "You live with your daughter, right?" which Plaintiff construed as an "implied threat to his daughter" a nonparty to the case. (Cmplnt., 4:2-9.) There are no allegations that Defendant Valdovino threatened to touch Plaintiff in a harmful or offensive matter or that there was a reasonable appearance he was about to carry out carry out an "implied" threat. Additionally, there are no allegations Defendant was put in imminent apprehension of such harmful or offensive contact. Consequently, Defendant's demurrer to the assault claim is sustained with leave to amend. To prevail on a claim for negligence, a plaintiff must show (a) a legal duty to use due care, (b) a breach of such legal duty, and (c) the breach as the proximate or legal cause of the resulting injury. (Ladd v.County of San Mateo (1996) 12 Cal.4th 913, 917.) "Breach is the failure to meet the standard of care." (Coyle v. Historic MissionInn Corp. (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 627, 643.) "A defendant does not owe a legal duty to protect against third party conduct, unless there exists a special relationship between the defendant and the plaintiff. In that circumstance, '[i]n addition to the special relationship . . . , there must also be evidence showing facts from which the trier of fact could reasonably infer that the [defendant] had prior actual knowledge, and thus must have known, of the offender's assaultive propensities. [Citation.]' In short, the third party's misconduct must be foreseeable to the defendant." (Doe v. Los Angeles County Dept. of Children & Family Services (2019) 37 Cal.App.5th 675, 682-683.) Plaintiff fails to allege any facts to demonstrate that a special relationship existed between him and Defendant. Plaintiff's Opposition does not address the demurrer to the claim of negligence. Thus, Defendant's demurrer to the negligence claim is sustained with leave to amend. Plaintiff's first amended complaint must be filed and served by February 20, 2025. (end of part 1, see part 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A. VS. LUIS M ARREOLA GARCIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Facts Be Deemed Admitted; Memorandum In Support Of Motion; Declaration In Support Of Motion
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 4, 2025, line 1, PLAINTIFF BANK OF AMERICA, N.A.'S Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Facts Be Deemed Admitted Bank of America N.A.'s unopposed motion to deem requests for admissions admitted is granted. The truth of any matters specified in the requests is deemed admitted in light of responding party's failure to respond to the requests for admissions before the date of hearing. See CCP 2033.280(b). CCP 2033.280(c) states that a court order for sanctions is mandatory in this circumstance, although Bank of America has not sought sanctions. The court orders defendant Luis M. Arreola Garcia to pay $50 to Bank of America within 20 court days of the date of notice of entry of this order. Bank of America may if it chooses waive payment of the sanctions. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE (MAURY BLACKMAN) VS. SUBSTACK, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 4, 2025, line 7, 3 - DEFENDANT SUBSTACK, INC. MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Hearing required to address sealing issues. Tentative: For the reasons stated in its order granting AWS's special motion to strike, the court grants Substack's motion. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)
